subtopic_detail: Methaemoglobinaemia in infant from topical anaesthetic
stem:
A 5-month-old boy is brought to ED 90 minutes after his mother applied a generous layer of an over-the-counter teething gel containing benzocaine to his gums every 30 minutes for the past 3 hours. She thought he was teething because of poor sleep. He has become progressively pale and dusky around the lips despite warming and looks slate-grey on inspection.

Nasal cannula oxygen at 4 L/min has not changed his appearance. He is alert.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 168, BP 84/52 mmHg, RR 56, SpO2 86% on 4 L/min nasal-prong oxygen (unchanged on 8 L/min), temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Slate-grey central cyanosis, no respiratory distress beyond tachypnoea, chest clear, capillary refill 2 seconds, alert with normal tone
data_table.Investigations: Arterial blood gas pH 7.36 pCO2 36 mmHg pO2 198 mmHg on 8 L/min, lactate 1.8; co-oximetry methaemoglobin level 32% (<2%), Hb 110 g/L (95-130); chest X-ray clear
data_table.Medications: Benzocaine teething gel (frequent application in last 3 hours)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum antibiotics for sepsis with cyanosis
options.rationale: He is afebrile, well-perfused, alert, with a normal CRP and lactate; the saturation gap between pulse oximetry 86% and pO2 198 on ABG is pathognomonic of dyshaemoglobinaemia, not sepsis. (Source: RCH CPG: Methaemoglobinaemia)

options.text: Administer methylene blue 1 mg/kg intravenously over 5 minutes
options.rationale: Methaemoglobinaemia above 30% with end-organ symptoms (or 20 to 30% with risk factors) requires methylene blue 1 to 2 mg/kg IV over 5 minutes. Co-oximetry confirms methaemoglobin (standard pulse oximeters read falsely around 85% as both forms absorb the same wavelength). Topical benzocaine in infants is a recognised AU and US public-health warning trigger. (Source: RCH CPG: Methaemoglobinaemia)

options.text: Administer ascorbic acid 200 mg intravenously and observe
options.rationale: Ascorbic acid reduces methaemoglobin slowly over hours and is reserved for patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency where methylene blue is contraindicated, or as second-line. With 32% met-Hb the slower reductant is not first-line. (Source: RCH CPG: Methaemoglobinaemia)

options.text: Increase oxygen to 15 L/min via non-rebreather and observe
options.rationale: Methaemoglobin cannot carry oxygen; raising FiO2 raises dissolved oxygen modestly but does not address the underlying defect. The persistently low pulse-oximetry reading despite high pO2 on ABG is the give-away. (Source: RCH CPG: Methaemoglobinaemia)

options.text: Arrange transfusion of one unit of packed red blood cells
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is third-line for refractory or G6PD-deficient methaemoglobinaemia where methylene blue and ascorbic acid are ineffective or contraindicated. Methylene blue is faster and avoids transfusion exposure. (Source: RCH CPG: Methaemoglobinaemia)

explanation.summary: Topical benzocaine is the classic AU paediatric trigger for acquired methaemoglobinaemia in young infants because of reduced NADH-methaemoglobin reductase activity. Clinical features are slate-grey cyanosis, refractory pulse oximeter saturation gap (Sp 85% with normal pO2 on ABG), and chocolate-brown blood. Treat with methylene blue 1 to 2 mg/kg IV.
key_points: Methylene blue 1 to 2 mg/kg IV is first-line above 30% met-Hb or 20 to 30% with symptoms
Pulse oximetry reads about 85% in significant methaemoglobinaemia regardless of true oxygen saturation - co-oximetry is required
Avoid methylene blue in G6PD deficiency - use ascorbic acid or exchange transfusion
explanation.pearls: AU consumer regulators have warned against benzocaine teething gels in infants under 2 years for exactly this reason. Other paediatric triggers include nitrites in deli meats, dapsone, prilocaine (EMLA), nitrate-contaminated water in bore-water rural settings.

subtopic_detail: Refractory primary PPH escalation - mechanical tamponade vs surgical
stem:
A 32-year-old G3P3 woman has delivered a live male infant by spontaneous vaginal birth. She had a precipitate second stage. Within 15 minutes of delivery she has had brisk ongoing bleeding from the genital tract. She has already received: oxytocin 10 IU IM, ergometrine 250 micrograms IM, oxytocin infusion 40 IU in 1000 mL Hartmann's at 250 mL/hour, tranexamic acid 1 g IV, and bimanual uterine compression for 10 minutes.

Vaginal and perineal lacerations have been sutured. The placenta is intact on examination. Estimated blood loss is now 1500 mL. She has no known medical conditions, no anticoagulants, no pre-eclampsia, and her blood pressure is currently stable on a unit of packed red cells running.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124, BP 96/62 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 98% on 4 L/min, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Uterus boggy and soft despite uterotonics, ongoing brisk red bleeding per vagina, no expanding haematoma, perineum repaired and dry
data_table.Investigations: Hb 86 g/L (115-160), platelets 154 x10^9/L (150-400), fibrinogen 2.4 g/L (2.0-4.5), APTT and INR within normal limits, fibrinogen falling on serial samples
data_table.Medications: Oxytocin infusion, tranexamic acid given, one unit packed red cells running

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Insert a Bakri intrauterine balloon and inflate to tamponade while preparing theatre
options.rationale: RANZCOG escalation for atonic PPH refractory to medical uterotonics (oxytocin, ergometrine, prostaglandin) and TXA is mechanical tamponade with a Bakri balloon while theatre is prepared. Balloon tamponade controls bleeding in about 85% of cases and buys time for surgical options if needed. B-Lynch and hysterectomy are surgical options reserved for tamponade failure. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Transfer to interventional radiology for uterine artery embolisation now
options.rationale: Uterine artery embolisation is an option only in haemodynamically stable patients where IR is immediately available and other measures have failed. With ongoing brisk bleeding and falling fibrinogen, tamponade and proceeding to theatre is faster than IR mobilisation. MTP activation may also be appropriate but is not the next surgical step. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Repeat ergometrine 250 micrograms intramuscularly with monitoring of blood pressure
options.rationale: Maximum total ergometrine dose is 500 micrograms in 24 hours; she has already had 250 micrograms so one further dose is technically allowed but unlikely to resolve a refractory atonic PPH and delays the next-line mechanical step. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Proceed straight to B-Lynch compression suture under anaesthesia in theatre
options.rationale: B-Lynch is the next surgical step if mechanical tamponade fails or in patients already having a laparotomy; jumping to laparotomy when balloon tamponade has not been tried bypasses a less invasive intervention that resolves the majority of cases. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Give intramuscular carboprost 250 micrograms and repeat once in 15 minutes
options.rationale: Carboprost is the next-line uterotonic when oxytocin and ergometrine fail, but with brisk ongoing bleeding past 1500 mL and continuing atony despite oxytocin and ergometrine and TXA, escalation to mechanical tamponade is the appropriate parallel step rather than further pharmacological delays. Carboprost remains an adjunct. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum Haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: RANZCOG PPH algorithm: medical uterotonics (oxytocin, ergometrine, carboprost or misoprostol) plus TXA first; then mechanical (bimanual compression, Bakri balloon); then surgical (B-Lynch, uterine artery ligation, internal iliac artery ligation, hysterectomy). IR embolisation is an option in stable patients with immediate access.
key_points: Bakri balloon controls atonic PPH in about 85% of cases as the bridge between medical and surgical management
Maximum 24-hour ergometrine dose is 500 micrograms (two doses)
Carboprost contraindicated in asthma; misoprostol 800 micrograms PR is the asthma alternative
explanation.pearls: Activate MTP early when blood loss exceeds 1500 mL with ongoing bleeding or fibrinogen falling below 2 g/L. RANZCOG includes a structured 4Ts mnemonic (tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin) - check that perineal and cervical tears have been completely repaired and that no retained placental tissue is missed.

subtopic_detail: Tubal ectopic with prior contralateral salpingectomy - surgical choice
stem:
A 31-year-old G3P1 woman, last menstrual period 7 weeks 2 days ago, presents with right iliac fossa pain and a small amount of dark vaginal spotting. She had a left tubal ectopic pregnancy 2 years ago treated by laparoscopic salpingectomy. She has been trying to conceive for the past 12 months and this is her first positive pregnancy test since the salpingectomy. She had a postpartum tubal contralateral preservation note in her previous operative report.

Her obstetric team has reviewed her. The on-call gynaecology consultant has had a detailed antenatal discussion of the choices with her partner and her present.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 86, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% in room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen with mild right iliac fossa tenderness, no peritonism, no rebound, cervical os closed
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 4200 IU/L, transvaginal ultrasound shows a 28 mm right tubal mass with no foetal cardiac activity, no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas, empty uterus, the left adnexa shows surgical absence of the tube as expected, anti-D negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Laparoscopic right salpingostomy with conservation of the tube
options.rationale: Salpingostomy in the standard case preserves fertility but in a woman with a prior contralateral salpingectomy this tube is her only remaining tube and recurrent ipsilateral ectopic in that same tube is the dominant risk. The benefit-risk balance flips - AU practice favours salpingectomy and IVF over a high-risk single retained tube. (Source: RANZCOG: Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Laparoscopic right salpingectomy with planning for future IVF
options.rationale: Standard teaching is that salpingostomy preserves fertility in women with one tube; however when the contralateral tube is already absent (prior salpingectomy), the affected tube is the only remaining tube and the risk of recurrent ipsilateral ectopic in that same tube after salpingostomy is dominant. AU practice favours salpingectomy in this T14-lever scenario because future fertility is best served by IVF rather than carrying ipsilateral recurrence risk in a single tube. (Source: RANZCOG: Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Methotrexate 50 mg/m^2 intramuscularly with weekly serial beta-hCG
options.rationale: RANZCOG methotrexate criteria require beta-hCG under 5000 IU/L AND mass under 3.5 cm AND no foetal cardiac activity AND no significant pain AND reliable follow-up AND no contraindications. She meets the laboratory threshold but has ongoing pain, which is a relative contraindication, and the surgical option is decisive. (Source: RANZCOG: Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Expectant management with serial beta-hCG every 48 hours
options.rationale: Expectant management requires a falling beta-hCG (usually below 1500 to 2000 IU/L) and a small asymptomatic ectopic. This patient has a 4200 IU/L beta-hCG with a 28 mm mass and active pain, none of which qualify her for expectant management. (Source: RANZCOG: Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy)

options.text: Laparotomy and total abdominal hysterectomy with right salpingectomy
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is a destructive option reserved for life-threatening haemorrhage from a ruptured cornual ectopic that cannot be controlled by lesser measures; the patient is haemodynamically stable with no free fluid and laparoscopy is appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG: Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Standard teaching that salpingostomy preserves fertility in women with one functional tube flips in patients with a prior contralateral salpingectomy: the affected tube is the only remaining tube and ipsilateral recurrence risk dominates. AU practice favours salpingectomy and IVF over a high-risk retained tube. This is the historical-bilaterality flip (T14 lever).
key_points: Salpingostomy is preferred for tubal ectopic in patients with a normal contralateral tube
When the contralateral tube is absent, salpingectomy is preferred because ipsilateral recurrence risk dominates
Methotrexate threshold for surgical management is beta-hCG ≥5000 IU/L (RANZCOG)
explanation.pearls: Anti-D 250 IU prophylaxis required for Rh-negative women with ectopic pregnancy. Methotrexate criteria are stringent: bhCG <5000, mass <3.5 cm, no foetal heart, no significant pain, no haemorrhage, reliable follow-up. The decision tree benefits from a documented antenatal discussion before theatre.

subtopic_detail: HELLP syndrome with thrombocytopenia - delivery and anaesthesia decision
stem:
A 28-year-old G2P0 woman at 34 weeks 2 days presents with a 2-day history of right upper quadrant pain. She has known pre-eclampsia treated with oral labetalol 200 mg three times daily since 32 weeks. Magnesium sulfate was loaded 1 hour ago and a maintenance infusion at 1 g/hour is running. Foetal heart rate is normal with reactive CTG.

Decision-to-deliver has been made and platelet count has dropped on serial samples over the last 6 hours. The on-call anaesthetic registrar has been called and the consultant obstetrician is in the unit.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 102, BP 165/110 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 98% in room air, temperature 36.8 °C, foetal HR 145 reactive
data_table.Examination: Right upper quadrant tender to palpation, hyperreflexic with 3 beats of clonus, no scotomata reported
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L (115-160), platelets 64 x10^9/L (150-400, was 110 four hours ago), LDH 820 U/L (<280), AST 240 U/L (<35), ALT 198 U/L (<35), bilirubin 38 micromol/L (<20), INR 1.0, fibrinogen 3.4 g/L (2.0-4.5), uric acid 0.46 mmol/L (<0.36), urinalysis protein 3+
data_table.Medications: Labetalol 200 mg PO TDS, magnesium sulfate infusion 1 g/hour

lead_in: What is the most appropriate anaesthetic and delivery plan?

options.text: Continue expectant management at 34 weeks with daily inpatient monitoring
options.rationale: Once HELLP is established with downtrending platelets, RUQ pain, and rising LFTs, delivery is the only definitive treatment. Expectant management between 34 and 37 weeks risks placental abruption, eclampsia, hepatic capsular rupture, and DIC. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer high-dose intravenous dexamethasone to delay delivery for 48 hours
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids for foetal lung maturity below 34 weeks may be given but they do not reverse maternal HELLP. At 34 weeks 2 days the foetus is past the steroid-benefit window and the mother's worsening HELLP requires immediate delivery, not delay. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Augment labour for vaginal delivery under epidural analgesia today
options.rationale: Epidural placement at platelet 64 with downtrending kinetics raises epidural haematoma risk; vaginal delivery may also take long enough that progression to lower counts intra-partum becomes more likely. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Proceed to category 2 caesarean section under general anaesthesia urgently
options.rationale: Platelet count of 64 x10^9/L with a downtrending trajectory (110 to 64 in 4 hours) is the kinetic warning that platelets may be below the regional anaesthesia threshold by the time the needle is placed. ANZCA / RANZCOG guidance uses a 70 to 80 x10^9/L static cut-off for neuraxial in obstetrics, with a strong caveat against placing a block in a downtrending count. General anaesthesia avoids the epidural haematoma risk in this trajectory. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Caesarean under spinal anaesthesia after platelet transfusion to above 100
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion to lift the count above 100 takes time, may be ineffective in HELLP (consumption is ongoing), and exposes her to transfusion reactions in a setting where regional may still not be safe due to platelet dysfunction beyond the number. AU current consensus is that GA is the safer pathway with a downtrending count. (Source: SOMANZ: Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: HELLP at 34 weeks with downtrending platelets and active maternal end-organ involvement mandates delivery. AU consensus uses a platelet count of 70 to 80 x10^9/L as the static threshold for neuraxial anaesthesia in obstetrics; a downtrending kinetic count is a stronger contraindication than a stable one above that threshold. General anaesthesia is the safer pathway in that scenario.
key_points: HELLP with downtrending platelets to under 80 x10^9/L is a contraindication to neuraxial - GA is preferred
Delivery is the only definitive treatment for HELLP
Magnesium sulfate prophylaxis against eclampsia is continued during and 24 hours after delivery
explanation.pearls: Hepatic capsular rupture is a rare but catastrophic HELLP complication suggested by hypotension and rising abdominal pain post-delivery; CT and emergency hepatic packing may be needed. Magnesium sulfate is continued 24 hours postpartum because eclampsia can occur in that window.

subtopic_detail: Twin-twin transfusion syndrome Quintero staging - laser timing
stem:
A 27-year-old G1P0 woman with a monochorionic-diamniotic twin pregnancy at 19 weeks 4 days is referred from her local foetal medicine unit. The amniotic fluid volume has changed rapidly over the last 2 weeks. She has been previously well.

The maternal foetal medicine consultant has reviewed the ultrasound and the staging discussion has just concluded. Both foetuses appear structurally normal.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% in room air, temperature 36.7 °C, foetal heart rates both reactive
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus consistent with 24 weeks size, no contractions, cervical length 32 mm
data_table.Investigations: Monochorionic-diamniotic twins. Twin 1 (donor): deepest vertical pocket 1.4 cm, empty bladder, umbilical artery Doppler with persistent absent end-diastolic flow. Twin 2 (recipient): deepest vertical pocket 10.4 cm, visible distended bladder, normal umbilical artery Doppler, normal ductus venosus a-wave, no hydrops

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Maternal sildenafil 20 mg three times daily to improve uteroplacental flow
options.rationale: Sildenafil for foetal growth restriction has been withdrawn from trials after concerns about neonatal pulmonary hypertension (Dutch STRIDER trial). It has no role in TTTS. (Source: RANZCOG: Twin and Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Selective foetal reduction of the donor twin by radiofrequency ablation
options.rationale: Selective foetal reduction is reserved for severe TTTS with a structurally abnormal twin or unfavourable laser anatomy, or for severe selective intrauterine growth restriction. With both foetuses structurally normal and stable, laser is the standard. (Source: RANZCOG: Twin and Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Foetoscopic laser photocoagulation of placental anastomoses
options.rationale: Twin-twin transfusion syndrome staged at Quintero 3 (donor with absent or reversed umbilical end-diastolic flow OR recipient with absent or reversed ductus venosus a-wave) and presenting between 16 and 26 weeks is best managed by foetoscopic laser ablation of placental anastomoses (eg Solomon technique). Laser is the only treatment that addresses the pathophysiology and is supported by AU foetal medicine centres for TTTS at Quintero stage 2 or above. (Source: RANZCOG: Twin and Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Serial amnioreduction every 1 to 2 weeks until viability is reached
options.rationale: Amnioreduction reduces maternal symptoms and preterm labour from polyhydramnios but does not address the underlying vascular anastomoses; the Eurofetus randomised trial showed laser has substantially better dual-survival and neurological outcomes than amnioreduction for Quintero ≥2. Amnioreduction is reserved for centres without laser access or after laser. (Source: RANZCOG: Twin and Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Conservative observation with weekly Doppler velocimetry studies
options.rationale: Quintero stage 3 has an untreated mortality above 70 to 90% without intervention; conservative observation is not appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG: Twin and Multiple Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Twin-twin transfusion syndrome is staged by Quintero criteria: stage 1 oligohydramnios in donor + polyhydramnios in recipient; stage 2 absent donor bladder; stage 3 abnormal Dopplers (donor UA AEDF or recipient DV a-wave reversal); stage 4 hydrops; stage 5 demise. Quintero stage 2 or above between 16 and 26 weeks should be referred for foetoscopic laser.
key_points: Foetoscopic laser is first-line for TTTS Quintero 2 or above between 16 and 26 weeks
Amnioreduction is symptomatic and reserved for centres without laser or as adjunct
Sildenafil has no role and was associated with neonatal pulmonary hypertension in STRIDER
explanation.pearls: After laser, twins are monitored weekly with biometry and Dopplers for the recurrence of TTTS or twin anaemia-polycythaemia sequence (TAPS), now reduced by the Solomon laser technique (equator-to-equator dichorionisation).

subtopic_detail: Postnatal depression severity and treatment escalation
stem:
A 32-year-old woman is 8 weeks postpartum after a forceps delivery and prolonged second stage. She presents to her GP for the routine 6 to 8 week postnatal check, where she completes an EPDS that scores 18 out of 30, with positive endorsement of question 10 about "thoughts of harming myself have occurred to me" recorded as "sometimes". She describes a 4-week history of low mood, anhedonia, poor sleep beyond the baby's feeding pattern, and decreased appetite with 3 kg weight loss. She is breastfeeding exclusively. She denies plans or intent to harm herself or her baby.

She has no prior psychiatric history and no history of bipolar disorder, postpartum psychosis, or postnatal psychiatric admission in herself or her family.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% in room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Mood low, affect reactive but constricted, no psychomotor retardation, no psychotic features, no suicidal plans or intent on direct questioning
data_table.Investigations: EPDS 18/30, item 10 positive (sometimes); TSH 1.8 mIU/L (0.4-4.0), Hb 124 g/L (115-160), ferritin 28 micrograms/L (15-200)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence diazepam 5 mg twice daily and continue to monitor
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines do not treat depression, accumulate in breast milk, may cause infant sedation, and risk maternal dependence. They are not first-line for PND. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health

options.text: Commence fluoxetine 20 mg orally daily and recommend formula feeding
options.rationale: Fluoxetine is not first-line for the breastfeeding mother because its long half-life and active metabolite norfluoxetine result in higher relative infant dose than sertraline. Stopping breastfeeding is also not required for SSRI initiation. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health

options.text: Refer immediately to mother-baby inpatient unit for admission
options.rationale: Inpatient mother-baby admission is reserved for severe PND with active suicidal intent or plan, psychotic features, or inability to care for baby. She has passive thoughts of harm without intent or plan and is functioning to a degree; community management with safety planning is appropriate. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg orally daily and refer for perinatal CBT
options.rationale: EPDS 18 with item 10 positive and 4 weeks of melancholic features defines moderate-to-severe postnatal depression. NHMRC and beyondblue guidance is for combined pharmacotherapy plus psychological therapy for moderate-to-severe PND. Sertraline is first-line for breastfeeding because of the lowest relative infant dose (<2%); the AU SAFEMOM database lists it as compatible with breastfeeding. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health

options.text: Refer for interpersonal therapy only and review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Psychological therapy alone is reasonable for mild-to-moderate PND (EPDS 10 to 13 without item 10 endorsement) but moderate-to-severe disease with passive suicidal ideation benefits from combined treatment. Waiting 2 weeks risks worsening with active suicidal ideation. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health

explanation.summary: EPDS ≥13 plus item 10 endorsement is the AU community threshold for further assessment and intervention. Moderate-to-severe PND benefits from combined pharmacotherapy (sertraline first-line in breastfeeding) and psychological therapy. Suicidal ideation requires a documented safety plan even when passive, and inpatient admission is reserved for active intent, psychosis, or care impairment.
key_points: EPDS ≥13 with item 10 endorsement is the AU community referral threshold
Sertraline is preferred SSRI in breastfeeding because relative infant dose is <2%
Combined SSRI plus psychological therapy beats either alone for moderate-to-severe PND
explanation.pearls: Always screen for bipolar history before starting SSRI in postpartum women - postpartum unmasking of bipolar with SSRI-induced mania is a recognised risk. EPDS item 10 is the suicide-question; any non-zero response warrants safety planning. Paroxetine has higher discontinuation symptoms; fluoxetine has the longest half-life and active metabolite.

sources.label: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health

subtopic_detail: First-episode psychosis - organic vs functional discrimination
stem:
A 22-year-old male university student is brought to ED by his parents with a 2-day history of new bizarre behaviour, paranoid statements that strangers are following him, and tactile hallucinations of insects on his skin. He has been recently treated with prednisolone 50 mg daily for 5 days for an asthma exacerbation, finishing 3 days ago. He has had no fever, no headache, no neck stiffness, and no overseas travel. His parents say he attended a music festival 1 week ago.

His usual medications include fluticasone-salmeterol inhaler and a recently completed prednisolone course. He has no other psychiatric history.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 118, BP 142/86 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 98% in room air, temperature 37.1 °C
data_table.Examination: Disoriented to date but oriented to person and place, fluctuant attention with inability to recite months backwards, mydriatic pupils 6 mm reactive bilaterally, dry warm flushed skin, no focal neurology, no neck stiffness
data_table.Investigations: FBC and CRP within normal limits, UEC and LFTs within normal limits, MRI brain normal, urine drug screen pending, blood ammonia within normal limits, TSH within normal limits, B12 within normal limits, CT head normal
data_table.Medications: Fluticasone-salmeterol; recent prednisolone 50 mg/day for 5 days (last dose 3 days ago)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Send urine drug screen for amphetamines, give lorazepam 1 mg IV, and observe quietly
options.rationale: Fluctuant attention, sympathomimetic features (mydriasis, tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, warm flushed skin), and tactile hallucinations point to delirium - probably from stimulant use at the music festival, not primary psychosis. RANZCP defines first-episode psychosis with these features as needing organic workup completed before functional diagnosis. Antipsychotics in acute stimulant intoxication may worsen hyperthermia; benzodiazepines are first-line. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Commence intravenous aciclovir 10 mg/kg for HSV encephalitis pending lumbar puncture
options.rationale: HSV encephalitis is a reasonable differential but he is afebrile with normal CRP, normal MRI, no neck stiffness, no headache, and an alternative cause is more likely from history; he also has the toxidrome of stimulants. Empirical aciclovir is not the most appropriate immediate step unless the LP is showing pleocytosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Encephalitis)

options.text: Diagnose corticosteroid-induced psychosis and stop the prednisolone now
options.rationale: Steroid-induced psychosis usually occurs during steroid therapy and at higher doses (typically above 40 to 60 mg prednisolone daily). The prednisolone course has already finished 3 days ago and the sympathomimetic features are not steroid-induced; this is a misattribution. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Commence olanzapine 10 mg orally and admit to psychiatry as first-episode psychosis
options.rationale: Diagnosing primary psychosis without completing organic workup is the AU sentinel-event lesson learned from missed encephalitis, delirium tropicale and stimulant toxidromes. Olanzapine carries QT prolongation and hyperthermia risk in stimulant toxicity. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Discharge home with his parents and follow up with his GP in 1 week
options.rationale: An acutely confused agitated patient with sympathomimetic features and disorientation is not safe for discharge. Acute toxic delirium has a measurable mortality if missed. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

explanation.summary: First-episode psychosis with delirium features (fluctuant attention, autonomic instability, hallucination modality other than auditory) mandates organic workup before functional diagnosis. Sympathomimetic toxidromes (amphetamines, cocaine, synthetic cannabinoids, MDMA) cause acute psychosis with mydriasis, hyperthermia, tachycardia and diaphoresis - treat with benzodiazepines, not antipsychotics, in the acute phase.
key_points: Tactile hallucinations and visual hallucinations are more suggestive of delirium / intoxication than primary psychosis
Sympathomimetic toxidrome treated with benzodiazepines first-line; avoid antipsychotics that worsen hyperthermia and QT
Steroid-induced psychosis usually occurs at >40 mg prednisolone daily and during, not after, the course
explanation.pearls: Always include a structured organic workup in first-episode psychosis: blood ammonia, B12, TSH, syphilis, HIV, autoimmune (anti-NMDA receptor antibody), MRI, EEG, and urine drug screen including synthetic cannabinoids. Ammonia derangement is the missed diagnosis in young men with new psychosis on valproate.

subtopic_detail: Adolescent anorexia nervosa - refeeding syndrome risk and admission criteria
stem:
A 15-year-old girl is brought to ED by her mother. She has lost 12 kg over the past 6 months and has been restricting food intake to about 600 kcal/day. Her current BMI is 14.8 (below 0.4 percentile for age). She admits to compensatory exercise (running 10 km daily) but denies purging. She has had no menses for 5 months. She is medically stable on initial review.

Her mother is concerned and supportive. The adolescent eating disorder team has been notified.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 42, BP 88/54 mmHg, RR 12, SpO2 98% in room air, temperature 35.6 °C, postural BP drop 22 mmHg from lying to standing
data_table.Examination: Cachectic, dry skin, lanugo, cool peripheries, no proximal weakness, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Na 138 mmol/L (135-145), K 3.1 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), Mg 0.62 mmol/L (0.7-1.1), phosphate 0.74 mmol/L (0.8-1.5), glucose 2.8 mmol/L (3.5-7.8), Hb 102 g/L (115-160), platelets 142 x10^9/L (150-400), LFTs ALT 86 U/L (<35), prolonged QTc 480 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with daily GP review and oral nutritional supplements
options.rationale: GCS is preserved but cardiovascular instability (HR 42, postural drop, QTc 480) makes home management with daily GP review unsafe. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg orally daily for comorbid depression
options.rationale: SSRIs are ineffective in underweight anorexia nervosa because of serotonergic depletion at low weight; antidepressant trial is deferred until weight is restored. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg orally daily for appetite stimulation
options.rationale: Olanzapine is not first-line in adolescent anorexia nervosa; weak evidence supports a small weight gain but it does not address the urgent metabolic and cardiac risks. Admission and refeeding are more critical now. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Refer to outpatient family-based therapy and review in 1 week
options.rationale: Outpatient management is contraindicated when medical-admission criteria are met. Family-based therapy is the appropriate longitudinal therapy but begins after medical stabilisation when she meets admission criteria. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Admit for cardiac monitoring and cautious refeeding at 20 kcal/kg/day
options.rationale: RANZCP eating disorder guidelines and the ANZAED guidelines have defined medical-admission criteria: pulse under 50, postural BP drop above 20 mmHg, hypothermia, prolonged QTc above 460 ms, glucose under 3.5, refeeding-syndrome risk markers (hypokalaemia, hypophosphataemia, hypomagnesaemia), and BMI under 75% of expected. She meets multiple. Start refeeding cautiously at 20 kcal/kg/day, correcting electrolytes before increasing calories. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Adolescent anorexia nervosa with bradycardia (HR under 50), postural BP drop above 20 mmHg, hypothermia, prolonged QTc above 460 ms, hypoglycaemia, or refeeding-syndrome risk markers (low K, Mg, phosphate) requires medical admission. Refeed cautiously at 20 kcal/kg/day with daily electrolyte checks. Family-based therapy is the longitudinal psychological therapy after medical stabilisation.
key_points: ANZAED admission criteria: HR<50, BP drop>20, hypothermia, QTc>460, BMI<75% expected, refeeding risk
Start refeeding at 20 kcal/kg/day; daily electrolyte and phosphate checks for first 5 to 7 days
SSRIs are ineffective in underweight anorexia - defer pharmacotherapy until weight restoration
explanation.pearls: Phosphate is the most commonly seen refeeding electrolyte to drop; prophylactic thiamine 200 mg IV before glucose load (Wernicke prevention) is standard. Family-based treatment (Maudsley method) is first-line psychological therapy for adolescents.

subtopic_detail: Adult ADHD pharmacotherapy with anxiety comorbidity and driving
stem:
A 28-year-old man is referred to a psychiatrist by his GP for new diagnosis of adult ADHD using DIVA-5 interview. He has a long history of inattention, distractibility, and forgetfulness. He works as a commercial truck driver and his employer requires fitness-to-drive review for the heavy vehicle category. He has co-existing generalised anxiety disorder partially controlled on escitalopram 20 mg daily. He has no history of substance misuse or cardiovascular disease.

His ECG and cardiology assessment have been completed by his GP.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 68, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% in room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Mood euthymic, mild restlessness, no tremor, no thyroid enlargement, no cardiac murmur
data_table.Investigations: ECG normal sinus rhythm with no QT prolongation, TSH within normal limits, FBC within normal limits, urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence long-acting methylphenidate after notifying medical assessor
options.rationale: AU Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive guidance allows commercial driving with stable, supervised stimulant medication for ADHD provided the medication has been stable for at least 4 weeks, the patient is not impaired, and an annual review by the treating psychiatrist confirms ongoing fitness. Long-acting methylphenidate is first-line for adult ADHD in AU. The medical assessor will need to be informed and follow Austroads guidance. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Commence dexamphetamine 20 mg twice daily without medical assessor notification
options.rationale: Notifying the medical assessor is part of the driver's and clinician's responsibility in AU under the Austroads framework; failing to notify breaches mandatory reporting requirements for heavy vehicle medical certification. Twice-daily dexamphetamine in a long-haul driver also risks afternoon waning. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Defer ADHD pharmacotherapy until he stops commercial driving altogether
options.rationale: AU practice does not require stopping commercial driving to treat ADHD; treatment may improve safety. The supervised-stable-medication pathway in Austroads is the standard. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Commence clonidine 50 micrograms orally twice daily as monotherapy
options.rationale: Clonidine is used as adjunct, often for sleep onset or as adjunct to stimulants in children. It is not first-line monotherapy for adult ADHD in AU. It is also sedating and could compromise commercial driving. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Commence atomoxetine 40 mg orally daily because of driving restriction
options.rationale: Atomoxetine is a second-line agent in ADHD with smaller effect size and slower onset (4 to 6 weeks). It is not contraindicated for commercial driving but stimulants are not blanket-prohibited; the Austroads decision is made on clinical stability rather than drug class. Atomoxetine also interacts with escitalopram via CYP2D6. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: AU first-line for adult ADHD is long-acting methylphenidate or lisdexamfetamine, started by a psychiatrist under Schedule 8 authority. Commercial driving is not contraindicated; Austroads requires stable medication, no impairment, and at least annual clinical review with notification of the medical assessor. SSRI-stimulant combinations are well tolerated; atomoxetine has CYP2D6 interactions with SSRIs.
key_points: Long-acting methylphenidate or lisdexamfetamine is AU first-line for adult ADHD
Austroads allows stimulant ADHD therapy with commercial driving subject to medical assessor notification and stability
Atomoxetine has CYP2D6 interactions with SSRIs; titrate slowly
explanation.pearls: Schedule 8 prescribing requires psychiatrist authority in most jurisdictions; check state-based S8 stimulant rules (NSW vs Vic differ). Cardiovascular screening before starting stimulants is mandatory; ECG only required if family history of sudden cardiac death or personal cardiac history.

stem:
A 52-year-old woman with recurrent unipolar major depressive disorder has failed two adequate antidepressant trials: sertraline 200 mg daily for 8 weeks and venlafaxine XR 225 mg daily for 8 weeks. She remains moderately depressed with PHQ-9 score 16 and has lost 5 kg over the last 3 months. She has had no manic or hypomanic episodes and her sleep is initial insomnia with terminal early-morning waking. She is not psychotic. Her thyroid function is normal.

She is currently on venlafaxine 225 mg and has reasonable adherence. Her psychiatrist is reviewing options.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% in room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Mood low, congruent affect, no psychomotor retardation, no psychotic features, MMSE 30/30, no manic features ever
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.9 mIU/L (0.4-4.0), B12 within normal limits, ferritin 78 micrograms/L (15-200), Na 138 mmol/L (135-145), random cortisol within normal limits
data_table.Medications: Venlafaxine XR 225 mg daily (current trial 8 weeks)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Add diazepam 5 mg orally three times daily for symptomatic relief
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines do not treat depression, risk dependence, and may worsen depression chronically. They are not first-line augmentation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Refer for psychodynamic therapy and stop venlafaxine immediately
options.rationale: Stopping the partially-effective antidepressant before establishing a replacement risks relapse; psychodynamic therapy has weaker evidence than CBT or IPT in moderate-to-severe depression and is not first-line for TRD. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Add aripiprazole 5 mg orally daily while continuing venlafaxine
options.rationale: Treatment-resistant depression (failure of 2 adequate antidepressant trials) is treated by either switching antidepressant class or augmentation; the strongest evidence for augmentation is with second-generation antipsychotics (aripiprazole, quetiapine, olanzapine, brexpiprazole). Aripiprazole 2 to 5 mg has the best metabolic profile and good evidence (CO-MED, AUS DOLPHIN). Lithium augmentation is also evidence-based but requires monitoring; lithium is preferred where there is a family history of bipolar. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Switch to a monoamine oxidase inhibitor with appropriate 2-week washout
options.rationale: MAOIs are effective in TRD but reserved for failure of multiple later-line strategies because of tyramine restrictions and serotonin syndrome risk if switched from serotonergic antidepressants without 2-week washout. Venlafaxine specifically needs 1 to 2 week washout before MAOI. They are not first-step augmentation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Add St John's wort 900 mg orally daily as augmentation therapy
options.rationale: St John's wort is a CYP3A4 inducer and serotonergic agent; combining with venlafaxine risks serotonin syndrome and unpredictable drug interactions. It is not an evidence-based TRD augmentation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Treatment-resistant depression (failure of 2 adequate antidepressant trials at therapeutic dose for 6 to 8 weeks each) is managed by augmentation with second-generation antipsychotic (aripiprazole, quetiapine, olanzapine) or lithium, or switching antidepressant class. ECT is considered for severe TRD or with psychotic features.
key_points: Aripiprazole 2 to 5 mg is well-tolerated TRD augmentation with the best metabolic profile
Lithium augmentation is evidence-based; preferred where there is family history of bipolar
ECT is indicated for severe TRD, psychotic depression, or active suicidality requiring rapid response
explanation.pearls: Always reassess for bipolarity (mood elevation history) before augmenting; missed bipolar accounts for some treatment-resistant cases. CANMAT and RANZCP guidelines list ketamine, esketamine, and electroconvulsive therapy as options for severe TRD. The STAR*D trial showed remission rates after 4 sequential steps approach 67%.
